name: Fogg Behavior Model (B=MAP)
overview: The Fogg Behavior Model (FBM), proposed by BJ Fogg at Stanford in 2009, states that a behavior occurs only when three elements converge at the same moment: Motivation, Ability, and a Prompt (B = MAP). When any one element is missing, the behavior fails. The model became the analytical core of Fogg's later book Tiny Habits (2019), which adds the formula Anchor + Tiny Behavior + Celebration as a practical recipe for embedding new habits into existing routines.
originator_or_company: Dr. BJ Fogg, PhD - founder and director of the Behavior Design Lab (formerly Stanford Persuasive Tech Lab, est. 1997) at Stanford University. Author of Persuasive Technology (2003) and the New York Times bestseller Tiny Habits: The Small Changes That Change Everything (Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, 2019). Coined the term 'captology' (Computers As Persuasive Technologies). Notable students include Mike Krieger (Instagram co-founder) and Nir Eyal (Hooked).
key_resources: [Fogg, BJ (2009). A Behavior Model for Persuasive Design. Persuasive '09 (ACM)](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1541948.1541999)
[Stanford Behavior Design Lab - Fogg Behavior Model resource](https://behaviordesign.stanford.edu/resources/fogg-behavior-model)
[Prompts in the Fogg Behavior Model (Spark / Facilitator / Signal)](https://www.behaviormodel.org/prompts/)
[Yu-kai Chou - BJ Fogg Behavior Model: S-Tier Designer's Guide (B=MAP)](https://yukaichou.com/behavioral-analysis/bj-fogg-extended-part-1-of-2/)
[Yu-kai Chou - BJ Fogg's 3 Triggers: Spark, Facilitator, Signal](https://yukaichou.com/behavioral-analysis/bj-foggs-triggers-extended-part-2-of-2/)
[Hermsen et al. (2025). Behavioral science meets public health: a scoping review of the Fogg behavior model. BMC Public Health](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12522219/)
[Stanford Behavior Design Lab - Ethical Use of Persuasive Technology](https://behaviordesign.stanford.edu/ethical-use-persuasive-technology)
[The Behavioral Scientist - The Fogg Behavior Model: B = MAP](https://www.thebehavioralscientist.com/articles/fogg-behavior-model)
core_mechanics: B = M x A x P, with all three required simultaneously (multiplicative, not additive - any zero kills the behavior). (1) MOTIVATION has three core motivator pairs operating on bipolar spectrums: Sensation (Pleasure / Pain), Anticipation (Hope / Fear), and Belonging (Social Acceptance / Social Rejection). Fogg considers Hope 'the most ethical and empowering' motivator and warns Fear is the most short-term effective but the most relationship-damaging when repeated. (2) ABILITY is reframed as Simplicity, gated by six factors - the chain breaks at the user's scarcest resource: Time, Money, Physical Effort, Brain Cycles (cognitive load), Social Deviance (norm conflict), and Non-Routine (deviation from existing patterns). Designers should make the behavior simpler, not the user 'more capable'. (3) PROMPT comes in three flavors matched to motivation/ability state: Spark (low motivation, high ability - prompt that motivates, e.g. inspirational copy or loss-aversion nudge), Facilitator (high motivation, low ability - prompt that removes friction, e.g. one-tap action or guided walkthrough), Signal (sufficient on both - simple reminder, e.g. calendar alert). Prompts can also be Hot (user can act NOW) or Cold (user cannot act now and must remember later). (4) ACTION LINE: a graph with Ability (X, hard->easy) and Motivation (Y, low->high) shows a curved threshold; behaviors plotted above the line succeed, below fail. The line is per-user and time-varying. (5) TINY HABITS METHOD (2019 book) translates the model into the recipe: 'After I [anchor], I will [tiny behavior]. Then I celebrate.' Tiny Behavior must be <30 seconds, requiring almost no motivation, so success depends entirely on Ability. The Celebration generates the positive emotion (Shine) that wires the behavior into automaticity through what Fogg calls 'emotions create habits, not repetition'.
designer_or_researcher_voice: BJ Fogg (behaviormodel.org): 'Behavior happens when Motivation, Ability, and a Prompt come together at the same time. When a behavior does not occur, at least one of those three elements is missing.'
BJ Fogg (Tiny Habits, 2019): 'Emotions create habits. Not repetition. Not frequency. Not fairy dust. Emotions.'
BJ Fogg (Tiny Habits, 2019): 'Help people do what they already want to do. Help people feel successful.' (the two maxims of Behavior Design)

BJ Fogg (Persuasive '09 paper): 'The FBM has implications for designers and researchers of persuasive technology. By understanding what drives behavior, designers can analyze why a behavior is not happening and intervene on the right element - not just throw more motivation at the problem.'

BJ Fogg (TED-Ed / Stanford lectures): 'You can never count on motivation. Motivation is a wave. The reliable path is to make the behavior easier - because Ability is the only lever you control consistently.'

BJ Fogg (Stanford Behavior Design Lab, on ethics): 'Persuasion technology is not neutral. Designers carry an ethical burden when they apply this model - the same techniques that help someone exercise more can be twisted into addictive products.'
metrics_or_evidence: The 2009 paper has accumulated 1,900+ academic citations (Google Scholar) with substantial scholarly engagement. A 2025 scoping review in BMC Public Health (Hermsen et al., PMC12522219) identified 6 health-intervention studies applying the FBM, of which 4 (66.6%) were RCTs (n=289 total participants, median 46). Reported effects include: HPV vaccination intention 63.3% -> 96.7% (p<0.001, Lee 2016); diabetes self-management - diet p=0.04, exercise p=0.005 (d=0.60), glucose p=0.02 (Sittig 2020); vaginal birth after cesarean intention +29% (p<0.05, Attarian 2021); gestational diabetes 10.34% vs 34.48% (p=0.028, Wang 2024). Tiny Habits self-reported figures (Fogg's coaching program, n=40,000+): ~80% adoption rate of tiny habits seeded in existing routines. NPR / Tiny Habits Academy reports >65,000 graduates of the free 5-day program. Limitations: small sample sizes, geographic bias (66.6% US-based), no long-term follow-up, no head-to-head RCT vs other behavior-change models (COM-B, HBM, TPB), and Celebration mechanism specifically lacks peer-reviewed validation.
criticisms: (1) The 2009 source is a conference paper, not a peer-reviewed journal article; the model is conceptually elegant but light on falsifiable mechanism. (2) Multiplicative form (M x A x P) is rarely operationalized as a measurable equation - most applications use it as a heuristic checklist, not a quantitative model. (3) The Action Line is illustrative rather than empirical - no published study has measured the actual threshold curve for any population. (4) Overlap with prior frameworks (Hull's drive theory, Triandis' theory of interpersonal behavior, Michie's COM-B) raises priority questions; FBM is sometimes accused of repackaging known constructs in catchier language. (5) Celebration as a core habit-formation mechanism (Tiny Habits) lacks RCT support; Lally et al. (2010) found habit automaticity took 18-254 days irrespective of Celebration-style reinforcement. (6) Cultural / individual variability in 'social deviance' and 'belonging' motivators is poorly specified - the model is implicitly Western-individualist. (7) Fogg's own students (Eyal, Krieger) productized the model into Hooked / Instagram, contributing to the attention-economy backlash now associated with Tristan Harris and Center for Humane Technology - putting the model under ethical scrutiny it did not anticipate.
failure_cases: (1) Pure-motivation campaigns (e.g. New Year resolution apps, anti-smoking shock ads) repeatedly fail because they ignore Ability and Prompt - the canonical FBM-cited failure mode. (2) Notification-spam apps (early Path, Foursquare swarm-era) treated Prompts as a substitute for Motivation, training users to disable notifications wholesale. (3) Wearable/health apps that dump dashboards full of metrics (high Brain Cycles cost) violate the Ability axis and show single-digit % long-term retention. (4) Corporate wellness programs with elaborate goal-setting (Marathon, walk 10,000 steps) fail when the Tiny Habits scaling rule is ignored - users abandon when the behavior is not <30s. (5) Fogg himself has publicly distanced from the captology / persuasive-technology framing after the 2018+ attention-economy backlash, acknowledging that some former students built products that 'crossed the line'. (6) The Stanford Persuasive Tech Lab was rebranded Behavior Design Lab partly in response to ethical concerns. (7) Hermsen et al. (2025) note no FBM-based intervention has been validated at scale (>1000 participants) or replicated cross-culturally.
antipatterns: (1) Using Fear as the sole motivator (loss-aversion only) - Fogg explicitly warns this damages the user relationship; classic dark pattern (Duolingo's 'sad owl' notifications, Snapchat streak loss). (2) Stacking M-x-A-x-P levers asymmetrically to compel rather than enable: high-Spark + low-Ability gates (e.g. variable-ratio rewards on top of streak loss aversion) cross into Means-Ends Reversal where the user pursues the prompt artifact, not the underlying value. (3) Applying Tiny Habits Anchor placement to install commercial behaviors users did not ask for (open the app after brushing teeth) - turns the model into a hook for engagement rather than user goals. (4) Using Signal prompts for behaviors the user is NOT motivated for or NOT able to do - pushes the user below the action line and trains habituation/dismissal. (5) Hot-Trigger spam (notifications when user can act now) without rate-limiting - Fogg explicitly warns of 'channel burnout'. (6) Ignoring Brain Cycles cost in B2B / educational apps - shipping multi-step onboarding wizards as 'just one more step' when each step is an Ability tax. (7) Treating Celebration as gimmicky animations (confetti, fireworks) divorced from genuine user-felt success - the underlying mechanism requires authentic positive emotion, not pixels. (8) Regulatory risk: EU DSA / UK CMA / Apple HIG explicitly call out 'manipulative engagement patterns' that descend from Fogg-derived persuasive techniques.
applicability_to_shikaq: Highly applicable as the analytical lens for ALL of shikaq's UX decisions, but the prescriptive Tiny Habits recipe must be heavily adapted to fit Apple HIG and the 20s-female cafe-iPad persona. Concrete translations: (a) Today screen morning prompt is a Signal (M+A both already high for a paid subscriber), not a Spark - copy stays calm, e.g. 'Today, 5 questions for ITパスポート' rather than guilt-trip. (b) Practice queue is engineered to maximize Ability: <30 second per question, single-tap answer, no decision tree branching - directly applies the Tiny Behavior rule. (c) Anchor strategy: ship onboarding flow that asks the user to map shikaq to an existing daily routine ('After I order my coffee, I will answer 3 ITパスポート questions'). Encode anchors in profiles.preferred_anchor and reference in morning notification copy. (d) Celebration: subtle haptic + a clean 'Nice. +5 minutes today' chip after completing the daily 3-ring close, NOT confetti animations. Aligns with Apple Fitness ring-close animation (calm authentic 'shine' moment). HEXAD mapping: Achiever (Ability and Signal pathway), Free Spirit (anchor customization), Philanthropist (the science-backed authenticity of 'we use behavior design that respects you'), Player (XP/streak surface ONLY if opt-in). (e) Prompt mix per persona: 70% Signal (calm reminder), 25% Facilitator ('Continue where you left off' resume button), 5% Spark (only when motivation is genuinely sagging and only with Hope-frame copy, never Fear). (f) Action Line awareness: Practice difficulty auto-adjusts so the user stays just above the line - too easy = Boredom, too hard = drops below - this is FSRS retention-curve territory in operational terms. (g) Strict no-Fear rule: shikaq's voice never threatens loss (no 'streak in danger!' red badges). The persona is a 20s woman who reads books in Starbucks - she will quit at the first guilt notification.
phase_assignment: Phase 1 (3 weeks). The model is the foundational design heuristic, not a feature - it must inform onboarding, Today screen, Practice queue design, and notification copy from day one. No code shipped, but every screen is reviewed against B = M x A x P. Specific Phase 1 deliverables: anchor-selection step in onboarding, Tiny Behavior <30s sizing rule for Practice queue, calm Signal-style notification copy template.
integration_points: No new tables required - FBM is design philosophy, not a feature. Light additions: profiles.preferred_anchor (text, e.g. 'after morning coffee'), profiles.reminder_time (time, single calm Signal/day default 08:30), profiles.allow_spark_prompts (bool, default false - Sparks opt-in only). Sidebar: copy review pass (no guilt copy). TodayContent: morning Signal banner reads from preferred_anchor + reminder_time. Practice screen: enforce <30s per question target in design review (Brain Cycles + Time axis of Ability). Notifications Edge Function: template library tagged by prompt type (signal / facilitator / spark) with strict cap rules - never 2 prompts/day, never Fear-frame copy. ai_qa_history: AI Q&A response style guide derived from FBM (Hope-framed, not Fear-framed). Onboarding flow: new step asking user to write their own anchor.
risks: (1) Theoretical fit gap: FBM is a heuristic, not a measurable equation, so there's no quantitative test that 'shikaq applied B=MAP correctly' - design reviews remain subjective. (2) Tiny Habits' Celebration mechanism is its weakest empirical leg; if shikaq's Phase-1 'shine' moment lands as cringe rather than authentic, it reverses the effect. (3) The model's most famous progeny (Hooked, Instagram, Snapchat streaks) are the very dark patterns shikaq must reject - applying FBM unguarded risks slipping into the same patterns. Mitigation: pair every FBM application with a Center for Humane Technology checklist gate (no Fear, no variable-ratio reinforcement, no manufactured urgency). (4) Cultural fit: 'Anchor in existing routine' assumes a stable Western-style daily routine; for some Japanese 20s users with shift work or commute-driven schedules, anchor selection may feel artificial - allow free-form anchors and skip-step. (5) Scope risk: model can be over-applied as 'all of UX = FBM', flattening other lenses (HEXAD, FSRS retention math, Apple HIG). Treat FBM as one of three coexisting design lenses, not a monoculture. (6) Founder authority risk: BJ Fogg's reputation has split since 2018 (Tiny Habits beloved / persuasive-tech criticized); some 20s-female users may have read attention-economy critiques and be wary of any 'behavior design' framing - don't market the model externally, use it internally only.
apple_hig_compatibility: compatible. FBM is principle-level; nothing in B=MAP forces a visual style. The Apple HIG values (Clarity, Deference, Depth, Less but better) align cleanly with Fogg's Ability axis (reduce Brain Cycles, reduce Time, reduce Physical Effort = Apple's reductive design ethos). Apple Fitness already implements an FBM-perfect loop: Signal (morning ring goal) + Tiny Behavior (close stand ring once an hour) + Celebration (close-ring haptic + chime). shikaq can borrow this template directly. The only conflict surface is if FBM is applied via aggressive Spark notifications or Hooked-style variable rewards - both rejected by HIG's 'avoid manipulative engagement patterns'. Use FBM, ship Apple-pure visuals.
ethical_score: neutral, leaning positive when applied with care, leaning negative when applied without ethical guardrails. Center for Humane Technology framework: FBM is the foundational vocabulary of both pro-user habit design (Tiny Habits, Stickk, health interventions) and the attention-economy compulsion stack (Hooked, Instagram pull-to-refresh, Snapchat streaks). The model is amoral by itself; outcomes depend on which axis the designer optimizes for and whose goal the behavior serves. shikaq's safe-zone application: optimize Ability (always pro-user), use Hope-frame Sparks sparingly, default to Signal, anchor in user-stated goals not platform metrics, never use Fear as primary motivator, cap notifications. Done this way the score is positive. Done carelessly (streak-loss notifications, variable-ratio rewards) the score collapses to negative.
evidence_quality: theoretical with growing observational and case-study support. The 2009 source is a conference paper (theoretical / conceptual). Tiny Habits supporting evidence is largely company case study (40,000 coachees self-report) plus the small set of FBM-grounded RCTs in the 2025 BMC Public Health scoping review (n=4 RCTs, total ~289 participants). No large-scale meta-analysis of FBM specifically. Adjacent constructs (implementation intentions - Gollwitzer 1999, habit formation - Lally et al. 2010, simplicity / friction - Thaler & Sunstein) have stronger RCT bases. Realistically: rigorous_RCT for adjacent components, observational_study for the FBM as a whole.
localization_fit_japan: high. The model maps cleanly onto Japanese habit-formation culture - the 'After I [anchor], I will [tiny behavior]' format is structurally identical to Japanese routine-building patterns (Radio Taiso anchored to morning broadcast, NHK Eikaiwa anchored to commute time, 朝活 anchored to coffee). 20s-female career-track audience already reads habit/productivity Japanese-translated bestsellers (Atomic Habits / Tiny Habits both top sellers). The model's emphasis on Hope over Fear and on calm gradual change resonates better with Japanese consumer-app expectations than the Hooked / Duolingo guilt-loop, which has measurable backlash in JP App Store reviews. Tiny Habits is published in Japanese (TJMOOK / Diamond, 2021) so the vocabulary already exists. Caveat: Fogg's specific Anchor recipe assumes individual-routine framing; for users in collectivist work environments, frame the Anchor as 'after my morning team check-in' rather than purely solo routines.
